basic_info.name: Polymer Additive Migration/Blooming
basic_info.name_ja: ポリマー添加剤移行・ブルーミング予測
basic_info.description: HSP distance between a polymer additive (stabilizer, plasticizer, flame retardant, antioxidant, UV absorber, etc.) and the polymer matrix is used to predict the tendency and rate of additive migration to the surface (blooming) or into contacting media. Smaller HSP distance indicates better thermodynamic compatibility and lower migration tendency. Larger HSP distance means the additive has lower solubility in the polymer matrix, creating a thermodynamic driving force for migration, surface blooming, or extraction. This is critical for food-contact packaging compliance, medical device biocompatibility, polymer long-term durability, and product aesthetics.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: Additive migration from a polymer matrix is driven by the thermodynamic activity of the additive in the polymer phase. When the HSP distance Ra between an additive and the polymer is large, the additive has limited solubility in the polymer, and any concentration above the solubility limit will phase-separate and migrate to the surface (blooming) or into a contacting medium. The Flory-Huggins chi parameter, estimated from Ra, determines the equilibrium solubility of the additive in the polymer. Below the solubility limit, migration is slow and diffusion-controlled; above it, the excess additive has high thermodynamic activity and migrates rapidly. For extraction into a contacting medium (food, body fluid), the partition coefficient between polymer and medium can be estimated by comparing Ra(additive-polymer) with Ra(additive-medium): the additive partitions preferentially into the phase with smaller Ra.
hsp_principle.key_equation: Ra = sqrt(4*(dD_add - dD_poly)^2 + (dP_add - dP_poly)^2 + (dH_add - dH_poly)^2); chi = V_add * Ra^2 / (6 * R * T); Partition coefficient K ~ exp((chi_add-medium - chi_add-polymer) * V_add / (R * T))
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: RED < 1.0 for good additive-polymer compatibility (low migration risk); RED > 1.2-1.5 indicates significant blooming/migration risk

required_inputs: HSP of the additive (delta_d, delta_p, delta_h)
HSP of the polymer matrix (delta_d, delta_p, delta_h)
HSP of the contacting medium (if evaluating extraction)
data_sources: HSPiP database for polymers and common additives
Group contribution methods for additive HSP (Van Krevelen, Stefanis-Panayiotou)
Published literature for plasticizers, antioxidants, UV stabilizers
Food simulant HSP values (water, ethanol, acetic acid, olive oil equivalents)
Experimental solubility sphere fitting for proprietary additives
complementary_parameters: Additive molecular weight and molecular size (affects diffusion coefficient)
Polymer glass transition temperature and crystallinity
Additive concentration relative to solubility limit
Surface area to volume ratio of the article

accuracy.limitations: HSP-based prediction captures thermodynamic compatibility (solubility) but migration rate also depends strongly on kinetic factors: diffusion coefficient of the additive in the polymer (which depends on additive size, polymer Tg, free volume, crystallinity). Crystalline regions act as impermeable barriers, reducing effective diffusivity but not changing thermodynamic driving force. At very low additive concentrations (well below solubility limit), migration is diffusion-controlled and HSP has limited predictive value for rate. Surface blooming involves nucleation and growth kinetics not addressed by HSP. Multi-additive systems may show synergistic or antagonistic effects on migration not captured by simple pairwise HSP analysis.
known_failure_modes: Low-molecular-weight additives with high diffusivity that migrate rapidly even when thermodynamically compatible
Additive aggregation or crystallization on the surface driven by surface energy effects rather than bulk solubility
Reactive additives that chemically bind to the polymer (e.g., reactive UV stabilizers) where migration is blocked regardless of HSP
Multi-layer structures where migration path involves multiple polymer phases
High-temperature processing that degrades additives, changing their HSP
accuracy.validation_method: Migration testing according to EU 10/2011 and FDA protocols (overall migration, specific migration), surface analysis (ATR-FTIR, XPS) for blooming detection, extraction studies with food simulants, accelerated aging at elevated temperatures, gravimetric loss measurements

key_references.author: Hansen, C.M.
key_references.title: Hansen Solubility Parameters: A User's Handbook, 2nd Edition

key_references.author: Begley, T.; Castle, L.; Feigenbaum, A.; et al.
key_references.title: Evaluation of migration models that might be used in support of regulations for food-contact plastics

key_references.author: Piringer, O.G.; Baner, A.L.
key_references.title: Plastic Packaging: Interactions with Food and Pharmaceuticals

literature.regulatory_relevance: Highly relevant to food-contact material regulations. EU Regulation 10/2011 on plastic food-contact materials requires specific migration limits (SMLs) for listed additives. FDA 21 CFR 170-199 governs food-contact substances. EU REACH requires assessment of substance migration for articles. HSP-based screening is accepted as a supporting tool for migration modeling in JRC (EU Joint Research Centre) guidelines. Commission Regulation (EU) 2017/752 references migration modeling approaches.

commercial_tools: Piringer model implementations in various software
